title: Minimalist Modern Nightstand
summary: A plywood-and-1x2-trim nightstand with a single drawer on Euro slides and an angled drawer face that doubles as a finger pull — no hardware needed.
description:
This nightstand is built the way a lot of mid-century case pieces are: plywood panels for the box, thin 1x2 trim wrapping every visible edge so no raw plywood ever shows, and a drawer that rides on cheap Euro corner-mount slides instead of anything fancier. The trim is doing real aesthetic work — it's what turns a plywood box into something with a finished, furniture-grade edge.

The drawer face is the one clever detail: rip the top edge at an angle rather than leaving it square, and that bevel becomes a built-in finger pull with no hardware to buy or install. It's a small table-saw operation that replaces a knob entirely.

Build the drawer box to the actual dimensions of the slides you bought, not to a number on this page — the front and back should measure 2-1/2" less than the opening, assuming your slides need 1/2" clearance per side, but confirm that against your specific hardware before cutting. Set the drawer face with a consistent 1/8" gap on all visible sides, which is what makes a plywood drawer read as considered rather than improvised.

One assembly trick worth keeping: leave the top panel unattached until the very end. A separable top is much easier to finish properly — brush every surface, including the underside — than a top that's already screwed down with three sides boxed in around it.

tools.note: Rips the plywood strips and bevels the drawer face's top edge for the finger pull.
Build the drawer box to your actual slide hardware's clearance requirement.

materials.name: Euro drawer slides, 16", bottom corner mount

cutList.note: Match this to your actual drawer slide length.
Measure and cut to fit — 2-1/2" less than your drawer opening, assuming 1/2" clearance per side on your slides.
Rip the top edge at an angle on the table saw — this bevel becomes the finger pull.

steps.title: Trim the side panels
steps.body: On each side panel, attach 1x2 trim with 1-1/4" brad nails and glue, keeping the outside edges perfectly flush to the panel edges. Do this on both side panels.

steps.title: Add the bottom rail and vertical trim
steps.body: Attach the bottom 1x2 rail to both side panels with 1-1/4" brad nails and glue. Measure and cut the vertical trim to fit, then attach the same way.

steps.title: Trim the top panel
steps.body: Trim the sides of the top panel with the 15-1/2" side rails, then trim the front and back with 1-1/4" brad nails.

steps.title: Add the cleats and set the top aside
steps.body: Attach the inside cleats with 1-1/4" brad nails and glue. Set the finished top panel on the base for now, without securing it — it's much easier to finish properly while it's still removable.

steps.title: Mount the drawer slides and shelf
steps.body:
Attach the drawer slides to the 10"-long 1x2 spacers.

Build the drawer box to fit your actual hardware: sides match your slide length exactly (16" for the slides called out here), and the front and back measure 2-1/2" less than the drawer opening, assuming 1/2" clearance per side. Assemble with 1-1/4" brad nails, the bottom with 5/8" brads. Test the slide action and adjust the hardware as needed.

Set the middle shelf on top of the 1x2 rails and attach with 1-1/4" brad nails.
materials: Euro drawer slides, 16", bottom corner mount

steps.title: Fit the drawer face
steps.body:
Set the drawer face on the drawer box with an even 1/8" gap on the sides and bottom, and attach with 1-1/4" brad nails. Further secure from inside with 1-1/4" screws.

Rip the top edge of the drawer face at an angle on the table saw before final attachment — that bevel is the finger pull, so no hardware is needed.

images.alt: Minimalist modern plywood nightstand with a beveled drawer face
